nativeRange.habitat: Generally found on coral, rocky, or rubble bottoms on reef flats, lagoons, and seaward reefs; recorded across a wide depth range of roughly 0–125 m.

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding primarily on small fishes, crustaceans (shrimp, crabs, krill), peanut worms, small gastropods, and octopuses in the wild; in captivity, generally advised to offer a varied meaty diet of mysis, krill, chopped shrimp, squid, clam, scallop, and similar marine-origin foods. Juveniles tend to have a high metabolism and have been reported to need four to five small feedings per day; adults are commonly maintained on two or more daily feedings. Tends to actively probe the sand and rockwork with its chin barbels, which has been documented to deplete in-tank microfauna quickly.

avoidWith: small fish that fit in the mouth

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso, 6243x4162. Tack-sharp in-water shot of a single adult in the golden/yellow phase with violet-blue back and blue-lined fins; whole fish framed, vibrant colour. Best striking pick.
Rickard Zerpe, 2348x1761. Sharp in-situ shot of the pale-bodied phase showing blue spots/lines, yellow caudal saddle, red eye and chin barbels; represents the second colour phase.
Diego Delso, 5227x5227. Group of golden-phase individuals on the reef showing the shoaling behaviour of this phase; slightly distant but colourful and correctly ID'd.

summary: A large, active Indo-Pacific goatfish that constantly probes the sand bed with its chin barbels. Generally not reef-safe with small mobile invertebrates and small fish, and has been reported as an expert jumper that requires a tightly fitted lid.
careNotes: The Yellow Back Goatfish (Parupeneus cyclostomus) is a large, active species that commonly reaches 35–50 cm and needs substantial swimming volume with a long tank footprint to thrive. Minimum tank size recommendations vary considerably across sources, from roughly 680 L up to 2000 L, and the species is generally considered best suited to very spacious show systems or public aquariums. A deep, fine sand bed is generally advised so the fish can use its chin barbels — the pair of sensory whisker-like appendages under its chin — to forage naturally; expect the substrate to be stirred and the water to cloud briefly after feeding. The Yellow Back Goatfish has been widely reported as an excellent jumper, so a tightly fitting lid or covered overflow is strongly advised. It tends to be peaceful toward similarly sized tankmates but is a documented predator on small fishes, ornamental shrimps, peanut worms, small crabs, and gastropods, so it is commonly considered reef-safe only with caution and unsuitable for tanks containing prized mobile invertebrates. Two distinct colour phases occur in the wild: a more common pale-bodied form with a striking blue-spotted dorsal pattern and a yellow saddle on the caudal peduncle (the narrow base of the tail), and a less common entirely yellow or golden phase — the form most often associated with the trade names 'Yellow Goatfish' and 'Yellow Back Goatfish'. Juveniles are most often seen in the yellow phase and have been observed to lighten from lemon-yellow to pale cream while actively feeding. Wild-caught specimens have been reported to commonly arrive with intestinal parasites and tend to benefit from quarantine and parasitic-worm treatment before introduction to the display tank.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Resolved from the collection landing page via search; the briefing's miscellaneous-fish collection URL did not list this species directly. Used for retail husbandry baseline (180 gal minimum, 72–78 °F, SG 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, peaceful, reef-safe with caution, moderate–difficult care).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum length (50.0 cm TL, common length ~35 cm), wild preferred temperature (24.5–29 °C, mean 27.8 °C), depth range (0–125 m), Indo-Pacific distribution, family (Mullidae), and order (Mulliformes). Used to extend the temperature range upward when averaging across sources.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (50 cm), temperature (~26 °C), salinity (SG ~1.026), reef-safe-with-caution rating, docile/shy temperament, and the recommended minimum tank size of 2000 L. Also confirmed the documented jumping/escape behaviour. Wider tank-size spread between this source and LiveAquaria is the basis for the needs_review status.

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (50 cm), temperature (23–27 °C tropical), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (SG 1.020–1.025), Indo-Pacific distribution, carnivorous diet, juvenile shoaling vs. adult solitary behaviour, advanced care level, expert-jumper behaviour requiring tight coverslides, and non-reef-safe rating.
